hook: The grandest imperial gate in Morocco, raised by a convert and finished after the sultan died
facts: Begun under Moulay Ismail and completed in 1732 by his son Moulay Abdallah.
Named for its designer Mansour al-Alj, the 'Victorious Apostate', a former Christian slave who converted.
Faced in green and white zellij with Koranic panels; flanked by marble columns taken from Volubilis.
Ceremonial entrance to the kasbah, not a defensive work.
legend: Legend says Moulay Ismail asked the architect if he could build better; when the man honestly said yes, he was supposedly punished or executed.
w: Begun by Moulay Ismail as homage to himself, finished by his son in 1732, and named for the convert who drew it. Roman columns from Volubilis prop a wall of green zellij and Koranic verse. Legend says the sultan asked the architect if he could do better; the honest yes supposedly cost him. Vanity, in stone.

hook: Colossal granaries with underfloor cooling, mislabelled the stables for 12,000 horses
facts: Vast grain store 182 by 104 metres, divided by 22 rows of thick arches.
Underfloor channels and metres-thick walls kept grain cool against siege.
Often called the royal stables, but the actual stables stood roughly 600 metres southeast.
Roof vaults left broken open, attributed to the 1755 Lisbon earthquake.
w: Twenty-two rows of fat arches built to outlast any siege, with channels under the floor and walls metres thick to keep the grain cool. Guides call it the stables; it stockpiled grain, the horses housed elsewhere. The 1755 quake cracked the vaults open to the sky. Imperial logistics, half roofless.

hook: An enormous still reservoir dug to water the palace through a siege
facts: Artificial basin roughly 319 by 149 metres, beside the Heri es-Souani granaries.
Fed by channels carrying water from springs south of the city.
Built under Moulay Ismail in the late 17th century to supply palaces and gardens.
Now a placid open-air lake locals walk beside at dusk.
w: A rectangle of still water the size of a small lake, dug so the imperial city could drink through a siege. Channels carried it from springs far to the south to feed palaces and gardens that mostly no longer stand. At dusk the surface goes mirror-flat and families drift its edge. Engineering, gone tranquil.

hook: The tyrant-builder's tomb, open to non-Muslims, watched over by clocks said to be a French king's apology
facts: Holds the tomb of Sultan Moulay Ismail, who ruled 1672 to 1727 from Meknes.
One of the few Moroccan royal mausoleums non-Muslims may enter.
Tomb chamber lined with zellij, carved stucco and a fountain courtyard.
Two grandfather clocks stand by the tomb, traditionally called gifts from Louis XIV.
legend: Legend says the clocks came from Louis XIV after he refused Moulay Ismail's proposal to his daughter, the Princess of Conti.
w: The sultan who built Meknes on forced labour lies here, in one of the rare royal tombs a non-Muslim may enter. Past zellij courtyards, two old grandfather clocks flank the grave. Legend says Louis XIV sent them after refusing to hand over his daughter, whom Moulay Ismail had asked to marry. A consolation prize.

n: Habs Qara (Prison of the Christian Slaves)
hook: Vast pillared cellars half-explored, draped in tales of captives lost in the dark
facts: Series of huge underground vaulted chambers beneath the kasbah, partly walled off and unmapped.
Popularly named for European captives, though scholars read it as a granary or storehouse.
Captive numbers were long exaggerated; estimates run to a few thousand at most.
Some bays remain sealed and have never been fully entered.
legend: Legend names a Portuguese prisoner, Kara, who designed it for his freedom; folk tales claim tunnels ran for miles and a captive once wandered in and was never found.
w: A forest of squat pillars holding up the dark, much of it still walled off and unwalked. Scholars read storehouse; the city reads dungeon for Christian captives. Legend names a Portuguese prisoner, Kara, who drew it to buy his freedom, and swears a man once wandered in and never came out. Believe what the cold suggests.

hook: The 'square of rubble', cleared by demolishing half the old city
facts: Name means 'the demolition'; laid out 1672 to 1674.
Moulay Ismail razed swathes of the medieval city to open it before his gates.
Used as a dumping and staging ground for pillaged building stone.
Now the medina's social heart, fronting Bab Mansour and the covered market.
w: The name means rubble, and it earned it: Moulay Ismail flattened a chunk of the old city to clear this ground before his gates, then piled it with stone pillaged from across Morocco. Today storytellers, grocers and evening crowds fill it, Bab Mansour glowering across. A demolition site that became the living room.

hook: A disgraced vizier's palace, confiscated and turned museum of Moroccan craft
facts: Built in 1882 by the Jamai family, grand viziers under Sultan Moulay Hassan.
Family fell from favour after 1894; the palace was seized and passed to the Glaoui.
French rule turned it into a hospital, then a court, then a museum in 1920.
Riad garden of orange trees, with carved cedar, stucco and zellij rooms.
w: A vizier's family raised this riad palace in 1882, then fell from favour and watched it seized, handed to rivals, made a hospital and a court before becoming a museum. Carved cedar, stucco and an orange-tree courtyard now frame Moroccan metal, wood and embroidery. Confiscated grandeur, repurposed for the public.

hook: A jewel-box Marinid college whose roof terrace looks over the medina's green tiles
facts: Founded around 1335 by the Marinid sultan Abu al-Hasan; restored under Abu Inan, whose name it took.
Courtyard ringed by two storeys of tiny student cells, once housing about 60.
Densely worked carved stucco, cedar and zellij around a central court.
Rooftop opens onto the green-tiled roofs and minaret of the Grand Mosque.
w: Marinid sultans raised this little college around 1335, ringing a tiled court with two floors of cells barely wider than a prayer mat, where sixty students once lodged. Carved cedar and stucco crowd every surface. Climb to the roof and the medina spreads out in waves of green tile. Scholarship, lived at close quarters.

hook: The northern gate of the medina, named for a vanished packsaddle market
facts: Northern gate of the Meknes medina, completed under Moulay Ismail around 1695.
Name traced to a market for packsaddles once held here.
Straight passage under a horseshoe arch, flanked by two square bastion towers.
Replaced an older Almoravid-era gate, traces of which survive to the south.
w: Two blunt bastion towers and a tiled horseshoe arch mark the medina's quieter northern edge, far from the tour buses. Moulay Ismail rebuilt it around 1695 over an older Almoravid gate. The name remembers a market for packsaddles long gone from the lanes behind. A working gate into a working quarter.

hook: The pavilion where foreign ambassadors knelt, later a tailors' uniform workshop
facts: Built late 17th century by Moulay Ismail as a reception hall for foreign envoys.
Interior split by two rows of arches into a basilica-like central aisle.
Later used to cut and sew military uniforms, giving its name 'cupola of the tailors'.
Stands on a terrace above the sealed chambers of the Habs Qara.
w: Here Moulay Ismail received European ambassadors, in a hall split by arches into a long central aisle, the sultan's power staged in stucco. Later it became a workshop stitching military uniforms, which gave it the homely name 'cupola of the tailors'. Below the terrace yawn the sealed cellars of the Habs Qara.

hook: The 'Thursday gate' into the old Jewish quarter, finely tiled and half-forgotten
facts: Completed around 1686 under Moulay Ismail; named for a Thursday market.
Once the western entrance to the mellah, the Jewish quarter laid out in 1682.
Moulay Ismail moved the Jewish population here beside his new royal citadel.
Spandrels filled with arabesque tilework and carved frames.
w: Moulay Ismail moved the city's Jews here in 1682, into a new mellah beside his citadel, and ran this tiled gate in as its western door around 1686. The name remembers a Thursday market. Arabesque spandrels and carved frames survive on a gate most visitors stride past. A community's threshold, quietly standing.

hook: Endless ruined arches that really did house the sultan's twelve thousand horses
facts: The genuine stables, roughly 600 metres southeast of the Heri es-Souani granaries.
Built to house the cavalry of Moulay Ismail, traditionally numbered at 12,000 horses.
Surviving rows of arches and walls, much roofless and overgrown.
A small mosque, the Jamaa er-Rouah, stood within the complex.
w: Where the granaries get the crowds, the real stables sit out beyond them, ruined ranks of arches running until they blur. This held the cavalry: twelve thousand horses, by the old count. Roofs long gone, weeds in the bays, a small stable mosque among them. Imperial scale, returned to the grass and the sky.

hook: Terraced century-old gardens on the slope between medina and new town
facts: Five hectares of terraces on the slopes above the Oued Boufekrane.
Laid out in the early 20th century below the medina ramparts.
Shaded by old palms and trees; the northern part scattered with columns from Volubilis.
Restored and reopened after works completed in 2024.
w: Terraces drop down the valley slope between the medina walls and the new town, shaded by palms a century old. Roman columns hauled from Volubilis lie scattered among the northern beds, casual as garden furniture. Freshly restored and reopened, it stays the place locals come to breathe. The city's green exhale.

hook: The medina's oldest anchor, hidden in plain sight among the souqs
facts: Friday mosque of the old city, thought to date from the 12th-century Almoravid period.
Expanded under the Almohad caliph al-Nasir, who brought it a new water supply.
Distinguished from above by rows of sloped green-tiled roofs.
Minaret rebuilt under Moulay Mohammed ben Abdallah in the 18th century.
w: One of the oldest things in Meknes hides among the souqs, doors easy to miss between stalls. Founded under the Almoravids in the 1100s, enlarged by the Almohads who piped it fresh water from miles off. Non-Muslims stay outside, but the cascade of green-tiled roofs and the high minaret give it away. The medina's quiet keel.

hook: A corner bastion of the kasbah walls, now a museum of Rif pottery
facts: Bastion tower built in the 17th century in the kasbah's rammed-earth defences.
Stands southwest of Bab Mansour, overlooking the mellah.
Defensive walls here ran 7 metres thick, doubled with a clear lane between.
Holds a pottery museum since 2003, with Rif ceramics from prehistory on.
w: A blunt corner of Moulay Ismail's defences, where rammed-earth walls ran seven metres thick and doubled up with a killing lane between. Since 2003 the bastion holds pottery instead of soldiers: Rif ceramics reaching back to prehistory, arranged inside the gloom. Climb up for the view across the mellah. War tower, turned vitrine.
